title: DCT vs DFT, Matrix Form & JPEG Pipeline

content.body:
**DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform):** uses both cosine and sine (complex exponentials). Produces real AND imaginary parts.

**DCT (Discrete Cosine Transform):** uses only cosines. Real numbers only. No imaginary part.

**How DCT avoids imaginary numbers:** It makes a **symmetric copy** of the input. Original 8 samples [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7] → DCT effectively works on 16 samples: [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7, 7,6,5,4,3,2,1,0]. When a signal is symmetric, the sine part of its DFT is always zero — only the cosine part remains. That is the DCT.

content.title: DCT vs DFT — Ramp Signal [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7]
content.body:
**DCT coefficients:** [9.90, −6.44, 0, −0.67, 0, −0.20, 0, −0.51]
→ Many zeros, energy concentrated in first 2 terms.

**DFT coefficients:** [28.00, −4.00, 9.66, −4.00, 4.00, −4.00, 1.66, −4.00]
→ Energy spread out — fewer zeros, harder to compress.

**Using only the first 3 terms:**
- DCT approximation → closely follows the original ramp ✓
- DFT approximation → oscillates badly, misses the shape ✗

**Conclusion:** DCT concentrates energy better → more efficient for compression → why JPEG and all major video codecs use DCT, not DFT.

**The 2D DCT is separable** — it can be computed as two consecutive 1D DCTs:
1. Apply 1D DCT to each **row**
2. Apply 1D DCT to each **column** of the result

This reduces iterations from 8×8 = 64 down to 8+8 = 16. That's why real-time video compression is computationally feasible.

content.label: Matrix Form of 2D DCT
content.expression: F = T \cdot f \cdot T^T \quad (\text{DCT: pixels} \to \text{frequency}) \qquad f = T^T \cdot F \cdot T \quad (\text{IDCT: frequency} \to \text{pixels})

content.body:
$$T[i,j] = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} & i = 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{2}\cos\!\left(\dfrac{(2j+1)\,i\,\pi}{16}\right) & i > 0 \end{cases}$$

**Key property — orthogonal matrix:** $T^T = T^{-1}$

The transpose equals the inverse. This is why the IDCT is just $T^T \cdot F \cdot T$ — no need to compute a full matrix inverse. Just transpose.

The DCT basis functions have two special properties:

**Orthogonal:** Different basis functions don't interfere with each other.
$$\sum_i B_p(i) \cdot B_q(i) = 0 \quad \text{when } p \neq q$$
Practical meaning: you can change one DCT coefficient without affecting any other coefficient.

**Orthonormal:** Orthogonal AND each basis function has length 1.
$$\sum_i B_p(i) \cdot B_p(i) = 1 \quad \text{when } p = q$$
This is what makes the DCT matrix orthogonal ($T^T = T^{-1}$) — and what keeps the IDCT simple.

content.title: The Full JPEG Pipeline
content.body:
**ENCODING:**

1. Split image into **8×8 pixel blocks**
2. Apply **2D DCT** to each block → 64 DCT coefficients
3. F(0,0) = DC (average brightness, biggest). Most high-frequency AC (bottom-right) are small.
4. **[LOSSY STEP] Quantize:** divide each coefficient by a quantization value and round. Small coefficients → 0. Larger quantization values → more zeros → more compression → more quality loss.
5. **Lossless encode** remaining non-zero coefficients with Huffman or Arithmetic coding (from Lecture 5)

**DECODING (reverse each step):**
1. Huffman decode
2. Dequantize (multiply back by quantization values)
3. Apply **IDCT** → reconstruct pixel block
4. Assemble blocks → reconstructed image

The loss is permanent — after quantization, the zeroed coefficients are gone and cannot be recovered.

**Must-know facts:**
- DCT concentrates energy better than DFT → fewer non-zero coefficients → better compression
- 2D DCT is separable: rows then columns, 16 iterations not 64
- Matrix form: F = T·f·T^T, inverse: f = T^T·F·T
- T^T = T^(-1) because basis functions are orthonormal
- JPEG lossy step: quantize DCT coefficients (divide + round → zeros)
- High compression = large quantization values = more zeros = more quality loss

flashcards.q: Why does DCT use only real numbers while DFT needs complex numbers?
flashcards.a: DCT makes a symmetric copy of the input signal. Symmetric signals have a zero sine component in their DFT — only the cosine part remains. That real-only cosine representation is the DCT.

flashcards.q: For the ramp [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7]: why is DCT better than DFT for compression?
flashcards.a: DCT coefficients: [9.90,−6.44,0,−0.67,...] — many zeros, energy in first 2 terms. DFT: [28,−4,9.66,−4,4,−4,1.66,−4] — energy spread out. Using only 3 DCT terms gives a good ramp. Using 3 DFT terms oscillates badly. DCT concentrates energy → fewer terms needed → better compression.

flashcards.q: What does 'the 2D DCT is separable' mean? Why does it matter?
flashcards.a: You can compute 2D DCT as two 1D DCTs: first across rows, then across columns. Reduces from 64 iterations to 8+8=16. This is why real-time video compression is computationally feasible.

flashcards.q: What is the matrix form of the DCT? What about IDCT?
flashcards.a: DCT: F = T·f·T^T. IDCT: f = T^T·F·T. T is the DCT matrix, T^T is its transpose. The IDCT is just transposing T — no need for a full matrix inverse.

flashcards.q: Why does T^T = T^(-1)? What property causes this?
flashcards.a: The DCT basis functions are **orthonormal** — orthogonal (different functions don't interfere) AND each has length 1. This makes T an orthogonal matrix, where the transpose equals the inverse by definition.

flashcards.q: What is the lossy step in JPEG? What controls quality?
flashcards.a: The lossy step is **quantization**: each DCT coefficient is divided by a quantization value and rounded. Small coefficients become zero. Larger quantization values → more zeros → higher compression → lower quality. The quantization values form a quantization matrix — different for each frequency.

flashcards.q: Describe the complete JPEG encode/decode pipeline.
flashcards.a:
Encode: split into 8×8 blocks → 2D DCT → quantize (lossy, zeros small coefficients) → Huffman encode.
Decode: Huffman decode → dequantize → IDCT → assemble blocks.
The quantization step is irreversible — zeroed coefficients are lost forever.

problems.q: In JPEG, what happens to image quality when you increase the quantization values (e.g., use Q=90 vs Q=10 where higher Q = larger quantization divisors)?
problems.a: Larger quantization divisors → more DCT coefficients round to zero → fewer coefficients survive → smaller file but lower quality. More high-frequency detail is lost, causing visible blocking artifacts on edges. Q=10 (high quantization) produces a much smaller file than Q=90 (low quantization) but at the cost of visible quality degradation.
problems.hint: Think about what happens when you divide a small number by a large number and round.

problems.q: Why does JPEG produce 'blocking artifacts' at high compression levels?
problems.a: JPEG processes independent 8×8 pixel blocks. At high compression, most DCT coefficients are zeroed, leaving only the DC (average brightness) and a few low-frequency terms per block. Each block reconstructs to a slightly different average with smoothed-out detail. The boundaries between blocks become visible as a grid of square patches — blocking artifacts. This is especially visible in smooth gradient areas.
problems.hint: Think about what happens at the edges of adjacent 8×8 blocks when only DC survives.
